planting.note: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep and 4-6 inches apart in well-drained to moderately moisture-retentive, humus-enriched soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; Spanish bluebells are vigorous and adaptable, thriving particularly well in partial shade beneath deciduous trees where they receive bright spring light before the canopy leafs out. Plant in large informal drifts for best effect and avoid mixing with sites near native English bluebells (H. non-scripta), as the two species hybridize freely and the resulting hybrids can threaten native bluebell populations.

directions: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep in well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade in fall for April-May bloom; Spanish bluebells are among the most vigorous and freely naturalizing of all spring bulbs, producing upright, arching stems bearing loose, one-sided racemes of pendant, lightly fragrant, bell-shaped flowers in shades of blue-violet, pink, or white above strap-like, bright green foliage. They are outstanding shade garden bulbs, naturalizing with exceptional ease under the canopies of deciduous trees and shrubs — conditions that suit them perfectly, as they receive full sun in early spring before the trees leaf out, then retreat into dormancy under dappled summer shade. Over time they spread by offsets and prolific self-seeding to form large, informal colonies that provide a reliable mid-spring floral display year after year without any maintenance. Note that Spanish bluebells hybridize readily with the rarer, native English bluebell (Hyacinthoides non-scripta) where the two grow in proximity; they should not be planted near naturalized stands of the native species.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2-3 inches deep and 3-4 inches apart in well-drained, moderately fertile, humus-rich soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; two-leaf squill is one of the very earliest spring bulbs to emerge and bloom, often flowering in February in mild climates or March further north, before most other bulbs have broken dormancy. It naturalizes freely and thrives under deciduous trees and shrubs, forming expanding colonies that bloom more prolifically each year.

directions: Plant bulbs 2-3 inches deep in well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade in fall for very early spring bloom; two-leaf squill is one of the earliest and most charming of the squill family, producing loose, arching racemes of intense, vivid blue star-shaped flowers — a singularly saturated, pure blue rarely matched among early spring bulbs — on short stems above just two (occasionally three) glossy, strap-like basal leaves, from which its species name derives. It blooms in February to March, often emerging through the last frosts of late winter alongside snowdrops and winter aconite, and thrives particularly well in the partial shade beneath deciduous trees and shrubs, where it colonizes the leaf-littered ground with a spreading carpet of brilliant blue. It naturalizes freely by both offsets and self-seeding, increasing steadily to form sizeable drifts over the years with no attention required. A choice companion for snowdrops, Eranthis, and early crocus in woodland-edge or naturalistic plantings.
sources: Chicago Botanic Garden Plant Evaluation Notes

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2-3 inches deep and 3-4 inches apart in well-drained, average to moderately fertile soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; spring starflower is tolerant of a wide range of soil conditions and partial shade, making it useful for naturalizing in difficult garden areas such as dry shade under trees, grassy banks, or the edges of paved surfaces. It spreads steadily by offsets to form dense, weed-suppressing clumps without becoming invasive.

directions: Plant bulbs 2-3 inches deep in well-drained, average soil in full sun to partial shade in fall for early to mid-spring bloom; spring starflower is a delightful, low-growing, easy-care bulb from South America that produces solitary, upward-facing, pale lavender-blue to silvery-blue, six-petaled, star-shaped flowers on slender stems above narrow, grass-like, bright green foliage that smells faintly of onions when crushed — betraying its relationship to the allium family. It blooms freely over several weeks from early spring, performing equally well in open sunny borders and in partial shade under deciduous trees and shrubs, where it tolerates surprisingly dry summer conditions once established. The bulbs multiply readily by offsets, slowly building into dense, attractive clumps that are excellent for edging, rock gardens, or naturalizing in rough grass and lightly shaded areas. The cultivar 'Wisley Blue' is a particularly fine selection with larger, deeper blue flowers.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4-5 inches deep and 6-8 inches apart in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; Sicilian honey garlic is extremely easy to grow in any well-drained soil and is highly adaptable to sun or partial shade. It self-seeds with some freedom and the persistent, papery seed heads are an attractive ornamental feature in their own right — deadhead after flowering if you wish to limit self-seeding.

directions: Plant bulbs 4-5 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in fall for late spring to early summer bloom; Sicilian honey garlic is among the most unusual and elegant of all the ornamental allium relatives — a tall, statuesque bulb producing long, strap-like basal leaves with a keel that smell of garlic when bruised, then in late May or early June sending up stiff, slender stems to 2-3 feet topped with large, loose, umbel-like clusters of 10-30 pendant, bell-shaped flowers in a subtle, sophisticated palette of cream, pale green, pink, and rich maroon-red, the colors shifting and blending from bud to flower to seed in a remarkable way. After the flowers fade, the seed heads turn abruptly upright on their pedicels, becoming stiffly erect papery capsules that persist on the stem and are highly valued for dried arrangements. It is an outstanding cut flower both in bud and in seed. The plant self-seeds modestly and slowly builds into a naturalized colony in suitable conditions; it is adaptable to poor, dry soils and thrives with minimal care.
sources: Kew Gardens — Plant of the Month

planting.note: Soak the small, irregular, dried tubers in water for 24 hours before planting — this step is essential, as dry Anemone blanda tubers absorb water very slowly and will establish poorly if planted without soaking; plant soaked tubers 2-3 inches deep and 3-4 inches apart with the concave (hollow) side facing up, in well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade, 4-8 weeks before first frost. Plant in large quantities for a naturalistic woodland effect and do not disturb established clumps.

directions: Soak tubers overnight, then plant 2-3 inches deep in well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in fall for very early spring bloom; Grecian windflower is one of the most valuable and versatile of all shade-tolerant spring bulbs — a low-growing, spreading, deciduous woodland tuber (technically a rhizome, not a true bulb) from the mountains of southeastern Europe and Turkey that produces daisy-like flowers 1-2 inches across in vivid blue, rich pink, or pure white with a bright yellow center, the petals reflexing in cold weather and closing at night, fully opening only when the sun reaches them. It blooms in March and April, carpeting the ground under deciduous trees and shrubs with jewel-like color at a time when little else is in flower. It is outstanding in deep or partial shade, naturalizing freely by both offset and self-seeding to form expanding colonies beneath trees where many other plants fail. Always soak the dried tubers for at least 12-24 hours before planting as they desiccate during storage and must be fully rehydrated to establish successfully.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep and 4-6 inches apart in moisture-retentive, humus-rich soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; despite its common name, summer snowflake blooms in spring (March-April), not summer — the name refers to the season of its native European habitat rather than its bloom time. It is outstanding planted beside ponds, streams, or in low-lying areas that stay moist, and naturalizes well in partial shade under deciduous trees where soil moisture is adequate.

directions: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep in moisture-retentive, humus-rich soil in partial shade or sun in fall for March-April bloom; summer snowflake is a taller and later-blooming relative of the spring snowflake (L. vernum), producing erect, hollow stems 12-18 inches tall that each carry a cluster of 2-5 pendant, white, bell-shaped flowers with a distinctive bright green dot at the tip of each rounded petal — giving the flowers a delicate, precise, spotted appearance that distinguishes them from all other white spring bulbs. Despite its misleading common name, it blooms in spring, typically in March and April alongside mid-season daffodils. It is a moisture-loving bulb by nature, native to wet meadows, riverbanks, and alluvial woodlands across Europe, and it thrives in conditions too damp for most bulbs — beside water features, in low-lying areas, or in partial shade where soil remains evenly moist. It naturalizes readily by offsets into large, robust clumps that increase year after year without division, and the selected cultivar 'Gravetye Giant' is a larger, more vigorous form with especially bold flowers.
